critique.summary: The article criticizes the use of scroll fade effects in web design, arguing that they are often overused, distracting, and poorly implemented. It highlights accessibility concerns, performance issues, and the lack of thoughtful design or testing behind these effects.
mainPoints: Scroll fade effects are frequently applied excessively and without subtlety, leading to a poor user experience.
The author finds scroll fade annoying both as a user and developer, often resulting in complicated JavaScript workarounds.
Accessibility concerns include vestibular disorders and cognitive overload, with prefers-reduced-motion media queries often ignored.
Scroll fade effects can negatively impact core web vitals such as Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), potentially harming SEO and site performance.
Proper implementation of scroll fade requires early planning, testing, and consideration of user experience, which is often neglected.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about scroll fade effects being overused and potentially harmful to user experience and accessibility are consistent with known web design best practices and accessibility guidelines. The mention of prefers-reduced-motion as a standard accessibility feature is accurate. The negative impact on performance metrics like LCP is plausible, as animations and JavaScript can delay content rendering. However, the article is largely opinionated and anecdotal, lacking empirical data or multiple sourced perspectives to fully substantiate all claims.
considerations: The article does not provide empirical data or studies quantifying the impact of scroll fade on performance or user engagement.
It does not explore potential benefits of scroll fade when used judiciously or with proper accessibility considerations.
The discussion on SEO impact is speculative and would benefit from referencing current search engine guidelines.
The article overlooks alternative animation techniques that might mitigate the issues raised.
User preferences and the diversity of user devices and contexts could influence the effectiveness and appropriateness of scroll fade.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the annoyance and accessibility issues caused by scroll fade and other scroll-related animations on websites. Several commenters share personal experiences with motion sickness and criticize excessive or poorly implemented animations, while a few defend the use of animations if done tastefully.

comments.author: wincy
comments.text:
Hah, the point has certainly been made. Absolute Barf-o-Rama.

I suffer from pretty severe motion sickness, which hasn’t really improved as an adult, and this page immediately made me feel like I’m going to throw up. Had to switch to reader mode after the first image. I was always the kid who couldn’t read in the car, and was always groggy on long road trips because of Dramamine (side note, Meclizine has significantly improved my life, as it has largely the same effect without drowsiness). As an adult I’m fine as long as I’m in the front seat, public transit is terrible for me. Elevators are tiny torture chambers, especially when stopping on multiple floors. And it’s cumulative, the sensation becomes worse the more I’m exposed to it over the course of a day (I have a mental “theme park budget” in my head of how many rides I can comfortably do!). VR can’t have any motion that isn’t firmly anchored to a sense of place (space ship/driving sims are okay though!)

I’m glad awareness is being raised about this, but I’m curious what websites are using this now? Is it just personal blogs and the like right now? I definitely would have noticed this cropping up on websites I frequent.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed personal account of motion sickness triggered by animations, highlighting a real accessibility concern that supports the article's critique.

comments.author: cogman10
comments.text:
Really, almost any animation or hijacking of scrolling should be abolished. It's one of the most disgusting things to encounter on a webpage.

I don't want your product to spin while I scroll down. I don't want animations or boxes to start appearing or disappearing. I don't want helpful tooltips, popups, or "I hope you enjoyed this" notifications to appear as I scroll.

What I want when I scroll is for the page to move, either up or down, in a completely consistent manner. I want to be able to reasonably predict what I'll see as I go up or down.

Apple loves this shit. Fortunately they aren't AS BAD as they once were, but you'll still encounter it on their product pages.

https://www.apple.com/macbook-neo/
comments.analysis: This comment articulates a common user frustration with scroll hijacking and animations, emphasizing predictability and usability, which aligns with best practices in UX design.

comments.author: sgbeal
comments.text:
The poster seems to be implying that this effect is prevalent across the web, yet i'm seeing it for the very first time on that post. (And, indeed, it's annoying. My eyes can't read when there's animation going on nearby.)

The goldfish animation along the bottom is epic and i will have to mine that bit for reuse somewhere :).
comments.analysis: This comment questions the prevalence of the effect but agrees on its annoyance, providing a balanced perspective on the article's claim.

comments.author: xnx
comments.text: It's amazing how web graphic designers don't realize 99% of all added motion/animation is just as annoying and unnecessary as <blink> and <marquee>.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a strong negative opinion on web animations, drawing a valid comparison to historically disliked HTML elements, though it is somewhat generalized.

comments.author: levmiseri
comments.text: I'm guilty of this as well. https://kraa.io/about has some fade-in animation for the intro text – driven by wanting the initial impression to be focused/minimal and 'unravel' as you go. I take it that most HN folks would vastly prefer to NOT have this?
comments.analysis: This comment admits to using fade-in animations intentionally for aesthetic reasons, providing a perspective from a developer balancing design and user experience.

comments.author: ryandrake
comments.text:
> This post purposefully ignores the reduced motion preference to give everyone the same truly terrible experience. I am sorry. Please use your browser’s reader mode.

"Reader Mode" shouldn't even be a special mode. It should just be the default browsing experience, and users who want all this styling crap should have to enable "Clown Mode" or something.
comments.analysis: This comment humorously advocates for simplified default browsing experiences, reflecting frustration with excessive styling but using hyperbole.

comments.author: yards
comments.text: I raise you one. Death to the parallax scroll. In fact, death to all scroll animations.
comments.analysis: A brief but emphatic statement rejecting all scroll animations, reflecting a common user sentiment though lacking nuance.

comments.author: sublinear
comments.text:
There are no bad animations, only bad designs.

If you design the animation to be way over the top like this, and then design the page to use it on every line then of course it looks like shit.

This is like arguing against any amount of sugar in food and then shoveling it into someone's mouth to try to prove your point. It's disingenuous and you aren't proving anything. I don't even think the top agreeing comments here are coming from web devs or the target users.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasoned defense of animations when used judiciously, arguing that poor implementation, not animation itself, is the problem.

comments.author: realityfactchex
comments.text:
I thought this was going to be about iOS and how now (as of iOS 26) there is a "fade out" at the top of every web page (around the notch/top-edge area).

When scrolling/reading a web page, it literally changes the section of the text that fades to gray.

So, "everything scroll fades".

I couldn't find a way to turn it off. Quite irritating, IMHO.

This article's critique seems valid, too (more generically about "scroll fade" in interfaces, e.g. web pages, which seems to mostly be about items appearing gradually via motion). Personally, I see less of that these days, compared to making every page in an OS fade out where unnecessary.
comments.analysis: This comment adds relevant information about a similar fade effect in iOS, broadening the discussion beyond web pages to OS-level UI design.

comments.author: marcosdumay
comments.text:
Gotta love the attention to detail at the end, that is illegible when selected too.

It's not realistic, though. Illegible sites never get that detail right.
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the realism and usability of the example site, pointing out practical issues with illegibility that undermine design quality.
